Browse Topics Search

Mapping, stats show scope of racial bias

Andy Lehren Dateline NBC February 2005
Data & DatabasesInvestigative MethodsDemographics & Census

The article investigates racial bias in policing through data analysis and mapping, focusing on a case in Cincinnati where a police pursuit led to the death of an unarmed black man, Timothy Thomas, and subsequent riots.

For a few days in April 2001, it was hard to miss news accounts about riots in Cincinnati. Daily stories outlined how police officers chased an unarmed 19-year-old Black man named Timothy Thomas. It was past midnight in the Over-The-Rhine neighborhood. A dispatcher called in officers to help pursue Thomas. The voice over the police radio offered a few details: a brief description of Thomas, and that he had 14 outstanding warrants.

An officer responding to the call spotted Thomas. He got out of his car and drew his gun, fired, and killed Thomas. Cincinnati rioters protested the police shooting, capturing headlines across the nation. Despite its national scope, the story focused on something simple: a traffic ticket. That's because Thomas' 14 outstanding warrants all stemmed from traffic tickets. Police, prosecutors and criminologists view traffic enforcement as a means of finding something else.

In this instance, the data revealed significant racial disparities in traffic citations issued, with Blacks being more likely to receive tickets for violations. By using this data, journalists analyzed patterns of racial bias in policing.
